explanation: The requirement states that whenever the ventilator is in the expiratory phase, the inspiratory valve (in valve) must be closed and the expiratory valve (out valve) must be open. This is best captured by a globally applied implication: globally (if 'expiratoryPhase' is true, then both 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' must be true at the same time). Thus, bool_exp2 is 'expiratoryPhase', and bool_exp3 is '(inValveClose & outValveOpen)'. The chosen template ensures that at every point in time, when expiratoryPhase holds, the required valve statuses are enforced.

The input states that during the expiratory phase (when expiratoryPhase is true), the inspiratory (in) valve must be closed (inValveClose is true) and the out valve must be open (outValveOpen is true). This is a safety or operational invariant that must be enforced at all times during expiration, so it is best captured by a globally (G) operator. The formula G(expiratoryPhase -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)) ensures that at all times, if the system is in the expiratory phase, then the in valve is closed and the out valve is open. This directly formalizes the natural language statement without reference to timing delays.

The input states that when the ventilator is in an expiration state (which is signaled by the atomic proposition 'expiratoryPhase'), the inspiratory valve must be closed ('inValveClose'), and the expiratory (out) valve must be open ('outValveOpen'). Thus, whenever 'expiratoryPhase' holds, 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' must also hold. This is best captured by the LTL formula: G (expiratoryPhase -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)), which says that globally (throughout all time), whenever 'expiratoryPhase' is true, both 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' are true at the same time. This directly enforces the requirement described in the natural language input.

The requirement "When the ventilator is in an expiration state the in valve shall be closed (pressure 0) and the out valve shall be open." means that whenever (globally, at all times) the system is in the expiratoryPhase, it must be the case that inValveClose is true (in valve closed) and outValveOpen is true (out valve open). The relationship is a direct implication: when expiratoryPhase is active, it should ensure the valve states are correct. Thus, template 10 (G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3)) is appropriate, with bool_exp2 as expiratoryPhase (expiratory phase active), and bool_exp3 as (inValveClose & outValveOpen) (in valve is closed and out valve is open). There are no further conditions or durations mentioned for the implication.

The requirement mandates that whenever the ventilator is in the expiratory state ('expiratoryPhase'), the in valve is closed ('inValveClose') and the out valve is open ('outValveOpen'). Therefore, the correct formalization is to enforce globally: if 'expiratoryPhase' holds, then 'inValveClose & outValveOpen' must also hold. The correct template for this implication is template 30, which is 'G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3)'. Here, bool_exp1 is 'expiratoryPhase' and bool_exp3 is '(inValveClose & outValveOpen)'. This LTL property captures the clinical requirement directly and succinctly.

The input statement specifies that whenever the ventilator is in the expiratory phase, the in valve must be closed and the out valve must be open. Using the provided atomic propositions, 'expiratoryPhase' indicates the expiratory state, 'inValveClose' stands for in valve closed (pressure 0), and 'outValveOpen' refers to the out valve being open. The specification is a safety property that must always hold: in every state, if 'expiratoryPhase' is true, then both 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' must be true. This property is best expressed using the LTL template 'G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3)', i.e., globally, whenever the condition (expiratoryPhase) holds, the guarantees (inValveClose and outValveOpen) follow. No time intervals are needed because this is a standard always-if property.

The natural language states that whenever the ventilator is in an expiration state (i.e., 'expiratoryPhase' is true), the in valve must be closed (inValveClose), and the out valve must be open (outValveOpen). These are strict requirements that should always hold whenever expiratoryPhase is true. This is best captured by a globally-scoped property that, whenever 'expiratoryPhase' holds, the conjunction of 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' must also hold. The implication G (expiratoryPhase -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)) expresses this requirement precisely.

The natural language says that whenever the ventilator is in an expiration state, the in valve must be closed and the out valve must be open. This translates to: always (globally), if 'expiratoryPhase' is true, then 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' must both be true. We represent this as G (expiratoryPhase -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)), which uses template 10. The N_DURATION is not needed for this template and set to null.

The requirement states that when the ventilator is in an expiration (expiratory) state, two conditions must always be enforced: the inspiratory valve must be closed ("inValveClose" true, which means inspiratory valve is shut so pressure is 0) and the out valve must be open ("outValveOpen" is true). This is a classic "whenever condition (expiratory phase), enforce properties (both inValveClose and outValveOpen)" safety requirement. Thus, we can express this as a global (G) invariant: whenever "expiratoryPhase" is true, both "inValveClose" and "outValveOpen" must also be true. In LTL, this is best represented using template 10: G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3), where bool_exp2 is "expiratoryPhase" and bool_exp3 is "inValveClose & outValveOpen".

The property requires that whenever the ventilator is in the expiratory phase (expiratoryPhase), both 'inValveClose' (in valve closed) and 'outValveOpen' (out valve open) must be true at all times. This matches the natural language requirement that when the system is in an expiration state, the in valve must be closed and the out valve open. This is captured with the template 'G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3)', where bool_exp2 represents the condition that 'expiratoryPhase' is active and bool_exp3 is the conjunction 'inValveClose & outValveOpen', expressing that both sub-conditions for the valve states hold. Thus, the system always ensures the correct valve configuration during expiration phases.
